slides.title: The Same Key Locks and Unlocks
slides.subtitle: Protecting Stored Data with Encryption: how a cryptographic algorithm and one shared key turn readable plaintext into scrambled ciphertext and back, and how AES does it on real files
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Welcome to Unit 5, Securing Applications and Data, and Lesson 5.3, Protecting Stored Data with Encryption. This is a single comprehensive deck covering the whole topic. Today we answer two questions: how does encryption protect a file, and how do real symmetric algorithms like AES actually do it? The big idea is right in the title — in symmetric cryptography, the same key both locks and unlocks the data. By the end you'll know the vocabulary of cryptography cold and be able to read an AES or OpenSSL scenario and explain exactly what's happening.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Explain how a cryptographic algorithm and a key turn plaintext into ciphertext and back, and why a larger keyspace resists brute force (LO 5.3.A)
Classify cryptographic algorithms as symmetric vs. asymmetric and block vs. stream (LO 5.3.A)
Apply the most common symmetric algorithm, AES, to encrypt and decrypt a file with the same key using tools like OpenSSL (LO 5.3.B)

slides.script: Three things to walk out with today. First, under learning objective 5.3.A, explain how encryption protects a file: an algorithm combines plaintext with a key to produce ciphertext, decryption reverses it, and a larger keyspace makes guessing the key by chance take longer. Second, still 5.3.A, classify any algorithm two ways — by how many keys it uses, symmetric or asymmetric, and by how it processes data, block or stream. Third, under 5.3.B, apply AES, the most common symmetric algorithm, to actually encrypt and decrypt a file with the same key, using a tool like OpenSSL. These map directly onto how the exam asks about cryptography.

slides.prompt: A laptop holding a company's confidential client files is stolen from a car. The thief has the physical disk and unlimited time. Yet the files turn out to be completely unreadable to him — even though he can copy every byte off the drive.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: what must have been done to those files so that having the disk is not enough to read them? What single secret does the thief still lack, and why does its size matter?
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own. A laptop with confidential client files is stolen — the thief has the physical disk and all the time in the world, yet the files are unreadable. In three to five sentences, explain what was done to those files so that having the disk isn't enough. What secret does the thief still lack, and why does the size of that secret matter? Hold your answers. By the end of class you'll be able to name that secret as the key, the process as encryption, and explain exactly why a bigger keyspace keeps the thief out.

slides.heading: What You'll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Guided Notes have three sections that match today's lesson. Green = CB required. Blue = enrichment.

sections.title: How Encryption Protects a File
sections.description: The purpose of cryptography, the plaintext → ciphertext pipeline, keyspace, and the symmetric/asymmetric and block/stream classifications (EK 5.3.A.1–A.5).

sections.title: Applying Symmetric Encryption with AES
sections.description: AES as the most common symmetric block cipher, its 128-bit block size and key-length trade-off, and the tools used to encrypt and decrypt a file (EK 5.3.B.1–B.4).

sections.title: Enrichment: Why Bigger Keys Cost Time
sections.description: How keyspace grows with key length and why longer AES keys are more secure but slower (EK 5.3.A.3, EK 5.3.B.2).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 5.3 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Three sections today, the first two green and CB-required, the last one a blue enrichment. Section one is how encryption protects a file: the purpose of cryptography, the plaintext-to-ciphertext pipeline, keyspace, and the two ways we classify algorithms. Section two is applying it for real with AES — the most common symmetric algorithm — its block size, the key-length trade-off, and the tools that actually encrypt and decrypt files. Section three is enrichment on why bigger keys buy security but cost time. Keep your pencil moving; I collect Guided Notes at the end of class.

slides.label: How Encryption Protects a File
slides.kicker: Protecting Stored Data with Encryption · Learning Objective 5.3.A
slides.script: Section one. Learning objective 5.3.A — explain how encryption can be used to protect files. We start with the purpose of cryptography and the core vocabulary, then keyspace, then the two classifications. This is the foundation everything else rests on.

slides.heading: The Big Idea: Hide the Information
slides.subheading: EK 5.3.A.1 and A.2 — cryptography hides information, and an algorithm combines plaintext with a key to make ciphertext.

left.title: Purpose of Cryptography (EK 5.3.A.1)
left.definition: Hide information; encryption hides, decryption reverses
examples: The purpose of cryptography is to hide information
A cryptographic algorithm defines a process for encrypting and decrypting information
Encryption is the process of hiding the information
Decryption reverses the encryption to retrieve the original information

right.title: Plaintext, Ciphertext, and the Key (EK 5.3.A.2)
right.definition: An algorithm combines plaintext with a key to produce ciphertext
examples: An encryption algorithm combines the information with a predefined key
The information to be encrypted is called the plaintext
The output of the encryption algorithm is called the ciphertext
Without the key, the algorithm cannot reverse the ciphertext

slides.footer: AP TIP: Keep the pipeline straight — plaintext + key → encryption algorithm → ciphertext, and the reverse for decryption (EK 5.3.A.1–A.2). The KEY is the secret that makes the process reversible only for the right person.
slides.script: Two columns set up everything else. On the left, EK 5.3.A.1, the purpose: cryptography hides information. The algorithm defines a process — encryption hides, decryption reverses to retrieve the original. On the right, EK 5.3.A.2, the vocabulary: the algorithm combines the information with a predefined key. The information you put in is the plaintext; the scrambled output is the ciphertext. Write the pipeline in your notes — plaintext plus key, through the algorithm, equals ciphertext, and the reverse to get back. The key is the secret that makes it reversible only for the right person; that's why losing the disk in the bell ringer wasn't enough.

slides.heading: Tracing the Pipeline: Plaintext to Ciphertext and Back
slides.context: Same key, both directions. Watch how encryption and decryption are mirror images of one another (EK 5.3.A.1–A.2).
rows: encryption algorithm with the shared key
slides.note: The same key is used in both directions — that is what makes this symmetric (EK 5.3.A.4). Take the key away and the ciphertext row is where an attacker is stuck.
slides.script: Let's trace the pipeline concretely. Start with plaintext — say, the message TRANSFER $5000. Feed plaintext plus the key into the encryption algorithm and out comes ciphertext, an unreadable string like 9F2A...C1. That ciphertext is what sits on the disk; with no key present, it's useless to a thief — that's the stolen laptop from the bell ringer. To get the message back, feed the ciphertext plus the same key into decryption, and you recover the original plaintext exactly. Notice the same key runs both directions — that's what makes this symmetric, and we'll name that classification in a moment.

slides.heading: Keyspace: Why Size Is Security (EK 5.3.A.3)
bullets: The keyspace is the number of possible keys that can be used in an encryption algorithm.
An adversary with no key must try keys to find the right one — essentially guessing.
The larger the keyspace, the longer it takes an adversary to discover the correct key by random chance.
This is why key length matters: more possible keys means a brute-force search takes far longer.
A large keyspace is what makes the stolen, encrypted disk safe even with unlimited time on weak hardware.
slides.script: Now keyspace, EK 5.3.A.3. The keyspace is the number of possible keys an algorithm can use. Think about the attacker who has the ciphertext but not the key — his only option is to try keys until one works, essentially guessing. The larger the keyspace, the more keys he has to try, so the longer it takes to hit the right one by random chance. That's the whole reason key length matters and the reason the thief in our bell ringer is stuck: even with the disk and unlimited time, a large enough keyspace makes guessing the key infeasible.

slides.heading: Two Ways to Classify Algorithms
slides.subheading: EK 5.3.A.4 and A.5 — classify by how many keys (symmetric/asymmetric) and by how data is processed (block/stream).

left.title: By Number of Keys (EK 5.3.A.4)
examples: Symmetric algorithms use the SAME key to encrypt and decrypt
Asymmetric algorithms use two DIFFERENT keys — one to encrypt, one to decrypt
AES, today's focus, is symmetric: one shared key both ways
Topic 5.4 introduces the second key of asymmetric cryptography

right.title: By How Data Is Processed (EK 5.3.A.5)
right.definition: Fixed-size chunks or one element at a time
examples: Block encryption handles data in fixed-size chunks called blocks
It produces an output block for each input block
Stream encryption handles input continuously
It produces output one element at a time

slides.footer: AP TIP: These two classifications are independent — an algorithm has BOTH a key count AND a processing style. AES is a symmetric block cipher (EK 5.3.A.4–A.5, B.2).
slides.script: Two classifications, and they're independent — every algorithm has both. On the left, EK 5.3.A.4, by number of keys: symmetric uses the same key to encrypt and decrypt; asymmetric uses two different keys, one for each direction. AES, our focus today, is symmetric. On the right, EK 5.3.A.5, by how data is processed: block encryption works in fixed-size chunks called blocks, one output block per input block; stream encryption flows continuously, one element at a time. Because these are independent, you can stack them — and AES, as we'll see, is a symmetric block cipher. Write both classifications with their EK numbers.

slides.heading: Don't Confuse Symmetric with Asymmetric
slides.misconception: All encryption uses one key to lock a file and a separate, different key to unlock it.
slides.correction: That describes asymmetric encryption. Symmetric algorithms — the model AES uses — use the SAME key to encrypt and decrypt (EK 5.3.A.4).
slides.whyItHappens: Students hear 'lock' and 'unlock' and assume two different keys, like a physical door versus a mailbox slot.
slides.example: Two coworkers share one secret key; either can encrypt a file and either can decrypt it with that same key — one key, both directions (EK 5.3.A.4).
slides.script: Quick misconception to lock down. Students often assume all encryption uses one key to lock and a different key to unlock. That's actually asymmetric encryption — two different keys. Symmetric encryption, the model AES uses, uses the same key both directions, EK 5.3.A.4. The confusion comes from the lock-and-unlock metaphor. Picture two coworkers sharing one secret key: either can encrypt, either can decrypt, with that single key. One key, both directions — that's symmetric.

slides.label: Applying Symmetric Encryption with AES
slides.kicker: Protecting Stored Data with Encryption · Learning Objective 5.3.B
slides.script: Section two. Learning objective 5.3.B — apply symmetric encryption algorithms to encrypt and decrypt data. We move from the concepts to the real tool: AES, where it's used, its block size and key trade-off, and how to actually run an encrypt and decrypt with OpenSSL.

slides.heading: AES: The Most Common Symmetric Algorithm (EK 5.3.B.1–B.2)
bullets: Computer-based encryption algorithms operate on binary data, not just letters.
The most common symmetric encryption algorithm is the Advanced Encryption Standard (AES).
AES secures Wi-Fi transmissions, internet browsing, file encryption on disks, and hardware-level encryption on processors.
AES is a symmetric key block cipher that encrypts data in 128-bit blocks (16 bytes).
AES can use keys of varying lengths; longer keys are more secure but take more time to encrypt and decrypt.
slides.script: AES, EK 5.3.B.1 and B.2. First, remember these algorithms operate on binary data, not just English letters. The most common symmetric algorithm is the Advanced Encryption Standard, AES, and it's everywhere — securing your Wi-Fi, your browsing, files on disk, even encryption built into processors. Mechanically, AES is a symmetric key block cipher: it encrypts data in 128-bit blocks, which is 16 bytes. And it can use keys of different lengths — longer keys are more secure but take more time to encrypt and decrypt. That trade-off is the part students most often miss; write it down.

slides.heading: Encrypting and Decrypting a File with OpenSSL
slides.context: EK 5.3.B.3–B.4 — symmetric encryption can be done on the command line. With OpenSSL the key is derived from the password you provide, and the SAME password is used both ways.
slides.note: Tools beyond the CLI: AES Crypt (open-source software) and many web-based tools also encrypt and decrypt files (EK 5.3.B.3). The -e flag encrypts, -d decrypts; everything else stays the same because the key is symmetric.
slides.script: Now the applied piece, EK 5.3.B.3 and B.4. Symmetric encryption can be done on the command line, with specialized software like the open-source AES Crypt, or with web-based tools. Let's do it with OpenSSL. To encrypt the file named test, you run openssl enc, dash-aes-128-cbc, dash-e for encrypt, in test, dash-k password, out test.enc — and the key is derived from that password. To decrypt, the command is nearly identical: dash-d for decrypt, in test.enc, the same dash-k password, out text. The only changes are the flag and the filenames; the password — the key — is the same, because AES is symmetric. Put in the wrong password and decryption fails. That's the whole loop.

slides.heading: Tools for Symmetric Encryption (EK 5.3.B.3)
slides.subheading: EK 5.3.B.3 — three families of tools that can all encrypt and decrypt files with a symmetric key.

items.title: Command Line (OpenSSL)
items.summary: On a command line interface, users encrypt or decrypt with OpenSSL, supplying a password from which the AES key is derived.

items.title: Specialized Software (AES Crypt)
items.summary: AES Crypt is an open-source tool that can encrypt and decrypt files without needing the command line.

items.title: Web-Based Tools
items.summary: There are many web-based tools for encrypting and decrypting files directly in a browser.

slides.script: A quick grid on tools, all from EK 5.3.B.3. The same symmetric operation can be performed three ways. One, the command line, where OpenSSL takes a password and derives the AES key from it — that's what we just walked through. Two, specialized software like AES Crypt, an open-source tool that encrypts and decrypts files without touching the command line. Three, web-based tools that do it right in the browser. The exam may name any of these, so know all three. Whichever tool you use, the symmetric idea is identical: one key encrypts and the same key decrypts.

slides.heading: Enrichment: Why Longer Keys Cost Time
slides.subheading: Not a new EK — it connects keyspace (A.3) to the AES key-length trade-off (B.2).

left.title: Bigger Key, Bigger Keyspace
left.definition: Each added bit doubles the number of possible keys
examples: Keyspace is the number of possible keys (EK 5.3.A.3)
Every extra bit of key length doubles that count
So a 256-bit key has a vastly larger keyspace than a 128-bit key
That makes brute-force guessing take dramatically longer

right.title: Security vs. Speed
right.definition: More security, but more compute time
examples: Longer keys produce more secure encryption (EK 5.3.B.2)
But they require more time to encrypt and decrypt
That is a real performance trade-off, not a free win
Engineers pick a key length that balances security with speed

slides.footer: TAKEAWAY: 'Longer is always better' is wrong. Longer keys grow the keyspace and resist brute force (EK 5.3.A.3) but cost compute time (EK 5.3.B.2) — it's a deliberate trade-off.
slides.script: Enrichment that ties the two sections together. Why does key length matter so much? Keyspace, EK 5.3.A.3, is the number of possible keys, and every extra bit doubles that number — so a 256-bit key has an astronomically larger keyspace than a 128-bit key, and brute-forcing it takes dramatically longer. But — and this is EK 5.3.B.2 — longer keys also take more time to encrypt and decrypt. So 'longer is always better' is wrong; it's a real trade-off between security and speed, and engineers choose a key length that balances the two.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 5.3.A–B)

terms.definition: The practice of hiding information; a cryptographic algorithm defines a process for encrypting and decrypting it (EK 5.3.A.1).
Encryption is the process of hiding information; decryption reverses it to retrieve the original (EK 5.3.A.1).
The information to be encrypted — the readable input to the algorithm (EK 5.3.A.2).
The scrambled output of the encryption algorithm (EK 5.3.A.2).
The predefined secret the algorithm combines with the plaintext to encrypt and decrypt (EK 5.3.A.2).
The number of possible keys an algorithm can use; a larger keyspace takes an adversary longer to break by random chance (EK 5.3.A.3).
Symmetric uses the same key to encrypt and decrypt; asymmetric uses two different keys (EK 5.3.A.4).
Block encryption processes fixed-size chunks called blocks; stream encryption processes input continuously, one element at a time (EK 5.3.A.5).
The Advanced Encryption Standard, the most common symmetric block cipher; encrypts data in 128-bit (16-byte) blocks with keys of varying length (EK 5.3.B.1, EK 5.3.B.2).

slides.script: Nine terms to capture the whole topic. Cryptography is hiding information; encryption hides and decryption reverses, both EK 5.3.A.1. Plaintext is the readable input, ciphertext is the scrambled output, and the key is the secret combined with the plaintext — all 5.3.A.2. Keyspace is the number of possible keys, 5.3.A.3. Then the two classifications: symmetric versus asymmetric by key count, 5.3.A.4, and block versus stream by processing, 5.3.A.5. And AES, the most common symmetric block cipher with its 128-bit blocks, 5.3.B.1 and B.2. Get all nine into your notes with their EK numbers — the exam tests these by name.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 5.3.A–B. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: A user runs a file through an encryption algorithm with a key. Name the readable input, the scrambled output, and the secret combined with the input — and cite the EK for each. Then state, with its EK, what classification it is if the SAME key decrypts the file.
Explain why an administrator might NOT simply choose the longest possible AES key. Reference both the keyspace benefit and the cost, and cite the two EK numbers involved.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: name the readable input — plaintext, EK 5.3.A.2; the scrambled output — ciphertext, also A.2; and the secret combined with it — the key, A.2. Then, if the same key decrypts, name the classification: symmetric, EK 5.3.A.4. Be precise and cite each. Question two: explain why the longest AES key isn't an automatic choice — a longer key grows the keyspace and resists brute force, EK 5.3.A.3, but takes more time to encrypt and decrypt, EK 5.3.B.2. That's exactly how the exam frames the trade-off. Go.

slides.heading: Protecting Stored Data, in One Slide
points: Cryptography hides information: an algorithm combines plaintext with a key to produce ciphertext, and decryption reverses it (EK 5.3.A.1–A.2).
Keyspace is the number of possible keys; a larger keyspace makes guessing the right key by chance take far longer (EK 5.3.A.3).
Classify algorithms two ways: symmetric (same key) vs. asymmetric (two keys), and block (fixed chunks) vs. stream (one element at a time) (EK 5.3.A.4–A.5).
AES is the most common symmetric block cipher — 128-bit blocks, varying key lengths, with longer keys more secure but slower (EK 5.3.B.1–B.2).
Apply it with OpenSSL, AES Crypt, or web tools; the SAME password-derived key encrypts and decrypts the file (EK 5.3.B.3–B.4).
slides.next: Topic 5.4 — asymmetric cryptography, where a second, different key is introduced so the same key no longer has to be shared to communicate securely.
slides.script: The whole topic in one slide. Cryptography hides information — plaintext plus key through the algorithm makes ciphertext, and decryption reverses it. Keyspace is the count of possible keys, and bigger is harder to brute force. Classify algorithms two ways: symmetric versus asymmetric by key count, block versus stream by processing. AES is the most common symmetric block cipher — 128-bit blocks, variable key length, longer keys more secure but slower. And you apply it with OpenSSL, AES Crypt, or web tools, where the same password-derived key works both directions. Next up, Topic 5.4: asymmetric cryptography, where a second, different key solves the problem of having to share that one secret key.

guide.title: Protecting Stored Data with Encryption — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Unit 5: Securing Applications and Data   •   LO 5.3.A–5.3.B
overview: Topic 5.3 is the symmetric-cryptography topic of Unit 5: students learn how encryption hides the contents of stored files and how a single shared key turns plaintext into ciphertext and back. They build the vocabulary of cryptography (algorithm, key, plaintext, ciphertext, keyspace) and the classifications of algorithms (symmetric vs. asymmetric, block vs. stream).

The applied half of the topic centers on the Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) — the most common symmetric algorithm — and on actually encrypting and decrypting a file with the same key using tools such as OpenSSL on the command line, AES Crypt, or web-based tools. It sets up Topic 5.4, where the second key of asymmetric cryptography is introduced.
los: LO 5.3.A — Explain how encryption can be used to protect files.
LO 5.3.B — Apply symmetric encryption algorithms to encrypt and decrypt data.
eks: EK 5.3.A.1 — The purpose of cryptography is to hide information. A cryptographic algorithm defines a process for encrypting and decrypting information. Encryption is the process of hiding the information, and decryption is the process of reversing the encryption to retrieve the original information.

EK 5.3.A.2 — An encryption algorithm defines a process for combining the information to be encrypted with a predefined key. The information to be encrypted is called the plaintext. The output of the encryption algorithm is called the ciphertext.

EK 5.3.A.3 — The number of possible keys that can be used in an encryption algorithm is called the keyspace. The larger the keyspace, the longer it will take an adversary to discover the correct key by random chance.

EK 5.3.A.4 — Cryptographic algorithms are classified by whether they use one key or two keys. Symmetric encryption algorithms use the same key to encrypt and decrypt information. Asymmetric encryption algorithms use two different keys—one to encrypt information and the other to decrypt information.

EK 5.3.A.5 — Cryptographic algorithms are also classified by whether they process information one bit at a time or in fixed-size chunks of bits. Block encryption handles information in fixed-size chunks called blocks, producing an output block for each input block. Stream encryption handles input information continuously, producing output one element at a time.

EK 5.3.B.1 — Computer-based encryption algorithms operate on binary data. The most common symmetric encryption algorithm is the Advanced Encryption Standard (AES). AES encryption is used to secure Wi-Fi transmissions, internet browsing, file encryption on disks, and hardware-level encryption on processors.

EK 5.3.B.2 — AES is a symmetric key block cipher that encrypts data in 128-bit blocks (16 bytes). AES can operate with keys of varying lengths. Longer keys produce more secure encryption but require more time to encrypt and decrypt.

EK 5.3.B.3 — Symmetric encryption and decryption can be performed using the command line, specialized software, or web-based tools. On a command line interface, users can encrypt or decrypt with OpenSSL. Specialized software like AES Crypt is an open source tool that can encrypt and decrypt files. There are many web-based tools for encrypting and decrypting files.

EK 5.3.B.4 — Using OpenSSL in a CLI, a user can encrypt and decrypt a file using commands where the encryption key is derived from the password provided. To encrypt a file named test with AES using a 128-bit key: openssl enc -aes-128-cbc -e -in test -k password -out test.enc. To decrypt the encrypted file using the same key: openssl enc -aes-128-cbc -d -in test.enc -k password -out text.
materials: Projector for cryptography vocabulary and OpenSSL command walkthroughs
Terminal or lab machines with OpenSSL (or AES Crypt) for the encrypt/decrypt demo
prereqs: Topic 5.1–5.2 understanding of securing applications and data and data classification
Comfort with binary data and the command line from earlier units

pacing.focus: Cryptography vocabulary, keyspace, and how encryption protects files (5.3.A)

segments.segment: Bell ringer — what would it take to keep a file unreadable if it were stolen
Direct instruction — purpose of cryptography: encryption, decryption, plaintext, ciphertext, key (5.3.A.1–A.2)
Keyspace and why a larger keyspace resists brute force (5.3.A.3)

pacing.focus: Block vs. stream encryption and AES fundamentals (5.3.A.5, 5.3.B.1–B.2)

segments.segment: Bell ringer — recall symmetric vs. asymmetric from Day 1
AES as the most common symmetric algorithm and where it is used (5.3.B.1)
AES block size and the trade-off of longer keys (5.3.B.2)

pacing.focus: Tools and applying symmetric encryption with OpenSSL (5.3.B.3–B.4)

segments.segment: Bell ringer — which tool would you use to encrypt one file
Tools for symmetric encryption: command line, AES Crypt, web-based (5.3.B.3)
OpenSSL encrypt and decrypt commands; same key both ways (5.3.B.4)
Guided lab — encrypt and decrypt a test file
Independent lab — encrypt, decrypt, and verify a recovered file
Whole-class review of the full topic

misconceptions.heading: Encryption and decryption need different keys
misconceptions.myth: All encryption uses one key to lock a file and a separate key to unlock it.
misconceptions.reality: EK 5.3.A.4 — that describes asymmetric encryption; symmetric encryption algorithms use the same key to encrypt and decrypt information, which is the model AES uses.

misconceptions.heading: Plaintext means unencrypted English text only
misconceptions.myth: Plaintext has to be readable words, and ciphertext is just scrambled letters.
misconceptions.reality: EK 5.3.A.2 and B.1 — plaintext is simply the information to be encrypted and ciphertext is the algorithm's output; computer-based algorithms operate on binary data, not just letters.

misconceptions.heading: A longer key is purely better with no cost
misconceptions.myth: You should always pick the longest possible AES key with no downside.
misconceptions.reality: EK 5.3.B.2 — longer keys produce more secure encryption but require more time to encrypt and decrypt, so there is a real performance trade-off.

misconceptions.heading: Keyspace is the size of the key file
misconceptions.myth: Keyspace just means how many bytes the key takes up on disk.
misconceptions.reality: EK 5.3.A.3 — keyspace is the number of possible keys an algorithm can use; the larger the keyspace, the longer it takes an adversary to find the right key by random chance.

support: Give students a labeled diagram of the pipeline: plaintext → encryption algorithm + key → ciphertext, and the reverse for decryption (EK 5.3.A.1–A.2).
Provide a two-column chart contrasting symmetric (one key) and asymmetric (two keys) and block (fixed-size chunks) versus stream (one element at a time) (EK 5.3.A.4–A.5).
Walk through the OpenSSL encrypt and decrypt commands line by line, highlighting that the same password-derived key is used both ways (EK 5.3.B.4).
extension: Have students explain, using EK 5.3.A.3, why doubling a key's length dramatically increases the keyspace and the time needed for a brute-force attack.
Ask students to research two real uses of AES from EK 5.3.B.1 (e.g., Wi-Fi or disk encryption) and describe what data each protects.
Challenge students to predict and verify what happens when they decrypt a file with the wrong password using OpenSSL, then relate it back to EK 5.3.B.4.

discussion: Why does a larger keyspace make an encrypted file harder for an adversary to crack by random chance?
When is symmetric encryption (one shared key) a good fit, and when does sharing that single key become a problem?
Why does the CED describe a trade-off for longer AES keys instead of saying longer is always best?
guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is pulled straight from the quiz on this page, so it always matches the questions students see.

quiz.title: Lesson 5.3 — Protecting Stored Data with Encryption — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 7 questions, 15 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: According to the CED, what is the purpose of cryptography?
options: To hide information so that it cannot be read without reversing the process
To compress information so that files take up less space on a disk
To copy information to a backup location in case the original is lost
To speed up information transfer across a network connection
questions.rationale: EK 5.3.A.1 — the purpose of cryptography is to hide information; encryption hides the information and decryption reverses it to retrieve the original. Compression, backup, and faster transfer are unrelated goals the CED does not assign to cryptography.

questions.stem: A user runs a file through an encryption algorithm with a key. According to the CED, what is the readable input called and what is the scrambled output called?
options: The input is the ciphertext and the output is the plaintext
The input is the plaintext and the output is the ciphertext
The input is the keyspace and the output is the cipher key
The input is the signature and the output is the digest
questions.rationale: EK 5.3.A.2 — the information to be encrypted is called the plaintext, and the output of the encryption algorithm is called the ciphertext. The reversed pairing is wrong, and keyspace, cipher key, signature, and digest are not the names for the input and output here.

questions.stem: A security team is choosing between two algorithms; one has a far larger number of possible keys than the other. According to the CED, why does the larger keyspace make that algorithm harder to break?
options: It makes each individual key shorter, so the algorithm runs faster on stored files
It guarantees that the same plaintext always produces a different ciphertext each time
It removes the need for a key entirely, leaving the adversary nothing to guess
It takes an adversary longer to discover the correct key by random chance
questions.rationale: EK 5.3.A.3 — keyspace is the number of possible keys, and the larger the keyspace, the longer it takes an adversary to discover the correct key by random chance. A larger keyspace does not shorten keys, guarantee varied ciphertext, or eliminate the key.

questions.stem: Two coworkers protect a shared file by using the exact same secret key to both encrypt it and later decrypt it. According to the CED, which classification of cryptographic algorithm are they using?
options: Asymmetric encryption, which uses two different keys for encryption and decryption
Stream encryption, which is defined by processing one element of input at a time
Symmetric encryption, which uses the same key to encrypt and decrypt information
Block encryption, which is defined by processing input in fixed-size chunks of bits
questions.rationale: EK 5.3.A.4 — symmetric encryption algorithms use the same key to encrypt and decrypt information, which matches the shared single key. Asymmetric uses two different keys, while block and stream describe how data is processed, not how many keys are used.

questions.stem: Consider these statements about classifying cryptographic algorithms from the CED. I. Asymmetric algorithms use two different keys. II. Block encryption handles information in fixed-size chunks called blocks. III. Stream encryption handles input continuously, producing output one element at a time. Which statements are accurate?
options: I and II only, leaving out the description of stream encryption
II and III only, leaving out the description of asymmetric algorithms
I and III only, leaving out the description of block encryption
I, II, and III, since all three describe accurate classifications
questions.rationale: EK 5.3.A.4 supports I — asymmetric algorithms use two different keys. EK 5.3.A.5 supports II and III — block encryption handles fixed-size chunks called blocks, and stream encryption handles input continuously, producing output one element at a time. All three are accurate.

questions.stem: An administrator needs the most common symmetric algorithm to encrypt files on disk and to help secure Wi-Fi. According to the CED, which algorithm fits, and what is its block size?
options: AES, a symmetric key block cipher that encrypts data in 128-bit (16-byte) blocks
AES, a stream cipher that encrypts data one single bit at a time as it arrives
A two-key asymmetric algorithm that encrypts data in 64-bit (8-byte) blocks
A password-only scheme that stores files without using any encryption key
questions.rationale: EK 5.3.B.1 — AES is the most common symmetric algorithm, used for Wi-Fi, browsing, disk file encryption, and processor-level encryption. EK 5.3.B.2 — AES is a symmetric key block cipher that encrypts data in 128-bit blocks (16 bytes). AES is not a stream cipher, not asymmetric, and does use a key.

questions.stem: A user encrypts a file with OpenSSL using openssl enc -aes-128-cbc -e -in test -k password -out test.enc. According to the CED, what is needed to decrypt test.enc back to the original?
options: A completely different second key, since AES requires separate keys to undo encryption
The same key (derived from the same password), used with the OpenSSL decrypt command
No key at all, since OpenSSL stores the recovery information inside the .enc file
A longer key than the one used to encrypt, to reverse the original operation
questions.rationale: EK 5.3.B.4 — to decrypt, the user runs OpenSSL with the same key, where the key is derived from the password provided (openssl enc -aes-128-cbc -d -in test.enc -k password -out text). Symmetric encryption uses one key, so no different, absent, or longer key is involved.
